g.data-narration: Welcome to Multithreading Models. We'll explore how modern operating systems manage concurrent execution through User and Kernel threads.
A process is a container for resources. Threads are active flows of execution within that process, sharing memory but keeping their own stacks.
There are two levels of threads. User threads live in application space and are fast. Kernel threads live in OS space and are managed by the system.
In the Many-to-One model, multiple user threads map to a single kernel thread. It's efficient, but if one thread blocks, the entire process halts.
The One-to-One model maps each user thread to a unique kernel thread. This allows true parallelism and non-blocking calls, but creating kernel threads is heavy.
The Many-to-Many model multiplexes user threads to a smaller number of kernel threads. It offers the best of both worlds: flexibility and concurrency.
The Two-Level or Hybrid model allows both Many-to-Many and One-to-One mappings, letting critical threads get dedicated kernel resources.
Thread libraries provide the API for creating and managing threads. The most common are Pthreads (POSIX standard), Windows API, and Java Threads.
Multithreading introduces system call complexities. For fork, does it duplicate all threads or just the caller? Most UNIX systems offer both options.
Signal handling is tricky. Should a signal like Ctrl+C go to one specific thread, all threads, or the thread that caused it?
Thread Cancellation terminates a thread before completion. Thread Local Storage gives each thread its own unique data copy.
In summary, One-to-One is the modern standard for true concurrency. Understanding these models helps you write efficient, scalable multithreaded applications.
g.text: User: Fast, low overhead. Kernel: True concurrency, OS managed.
Handle fork() carefully, manage signals, prefer deferred cancellation.
